modules.title: Build Your Learning System
modules.why: Intermediate interviews demand recall, synthesis, and calm reasoning under pressure. Passive recognition is not enough.
objectives: Create a study loop driven by weak areas
Use retrieval and spaced review instead of rereading
Define observable evidence of interview readiness

concepts.title: Retrieval before review
concepts.body: Attempt an explanation or solution before opening notes. The gap between your attempt and the reference is the useful learning signal.

concepts.title: Interleave the bar
concepts.body: Mix coding, systems, security design, investigation, and behavioral practice every week so one comfortable domain cannot hide another.

concepts.title: Track evidence
concepts.body: A topic is ready when you can explain it cold, apply it to a novel scenario, handle a follow-up, and name tradeoffs.

modules.example: Instead of rereading DNS, close the notes and trace a request aloud, diagnose a tunneling alert, then write a detector. Record exactly where the explanation failed.
pitfalls: Counting reading time as mastery
Studying favorite topics while avoiding coding or systems gaps

lab.title: Create a readiness baseline
lab.scenario: You have four weeks before a mixed security-engineering loop.
deliverables: Score yourself from 0-3 in coding, systems, application security, cloud, detection, and incident response
Write one piece of evidence supporting every score
Create a seven-day schedule that spends the most time on the two lowest scores

interview.prompt: Tell me how you would prepare for an unfamiliar security domain in two weeks.
rubric: Defines the target role and interview signals
Uses retrieval, practice, and feedback
Prioritizes weak areas
Measures progress with observable outputs

quiz.prompt: Which activity gives the strongest evidence that a topic is interview-ready?
options: Recognizing all terms in a glossary
Explaining and applying it cold to a new scenario
quiz.explanation: Recall plus transfer to a novel scenario is stronger evidence than recognition.

quiz.prompt: What should determine the first topic in a study session?
options: The module with the most links
quiz.explanation: Role relevance and demonstrated weakness should drive priority.

modules.title: Networking and Traffic Reasoning
modules.why: Network facts connect user actions, host behavior, service boundaries, and attacker infrastructure.
objectives: Trace traffic from DNS through TLS and HTTP
Interpret TCP, UDP, routing, and common protocol evidence
Distinguish normal traffic from reconnaissance and exfiltration

concepts.title: Follow the packet
concepts.body: Reason layer by layer: name resolution, route, transport state, encrypted session, application request, and response. At each layer ask what can be logged or spoofed.

concepts.title: State creates evidence
concepts.body: TCP handshakes, resolver queries, proxy requests, and flow records expose different facts. No single telemetry source tells the whole story.

concepts.title: Shape matters
concepts.body: Beacon intervals, fan-out, failed handshakes, byte ratios, entropy, and destination rarity often matter more than one IP address.

modules.example: Long high-entropy subdomains sent every sixty seconds suggest DNS tunneling. Validate with resolver logs, query length and uniqueness, host process telemetry, and egress volume before containing.
pitfalls: Treating ports as proof of application protocol
Assuming an IP address reliably identifies a person or physical location

lab.title: Triage suspicious DNS
lab.scenario: A workstation sends 400 unique subdomains to one newly observed domain during an hour.
deliverables: List the DNS, network, and endpoint fields you need
Write detection pseudocode with a threshold and time window
Name three benign causes and how you would eliminate them

interview.prompt: Design a detector for internal port scanning without blocking legitimate administration.
rubric: Clarifies available telemetry and network shape
Defines source, destination, window, and threshold
Handles distributed and slow scans
Discusses baselines, allowlists, and false positives

quiz.prompt: Which signal most directly supports a TCP SYN scan hypothesis?
options: Many completed TLS sessions to one host
One source sends SYNs to many ports with few completed handshakes
Large outbound UDP payloads to one resolver
quiz.explanation: Broad SYN attempts with few completed handshakes are characteristic scan evidence.

quiz.prompt: Why combine DNS and endpoint telemetry during suspected DNS exfiltration?
options: Endpoint data can identify the process generating suspicious queries
Endpoint data decrypts all DNS traffic
quiz.explanation: Resolver telemetry shows the query; endpoint telemetry can attribute it to a process and user context.

modules.title: Operating Systems and Host Boundaries
modules.why: Processes, memory, permissions, filesystems, and kernels define what exploitation and host investigation actually mean.
objectives: Explain user/kernel boundaries and process execution
Compare discretionary and mandatory access controls
Identify useful Linux, Windows, and macOS artifacts

concepts.title: Authority follows context
concepts.body: A process acts with an identity, token, capabilities, namespaces, environment, open handles, and inherited resources. Compromise impact depends on that context.

concepts.title: Isolation is layered
concepts.body: Permissions, ACLs, MAC policy, sandboxing, memory protections, and least privilege reduce different paths; no one control is a complete boundary.

concepts.title: Execution leaves artifacts
concepts.body: Process ancestry, command lines, loaded modules, file metadata, persistence locations, and authentication logs help reconstruct host activity.

modules.example: A web process writing to a privileged startup location suggests both application compromise and a broken host boundary. Investigate process ancestry, token privileges, file metadata, and persistence execution.
pitfalls: Equating administrator access with kernel execution
Naming tools without explaining the artifact or question they answer

lab.title: Host compromise evidence map
lab.scenario: An internet-facing Linux service spawned a shell and wrote a scheduled task.
deliverables: Draw the process and privilege chain
List volatile and durable artifacts in collection order
Recommend three controls mapped to distinct failure points

interview.prompt: A service running as root is exploited. Explain impact, investigation, and prevention.
rubric: Separates initial execution from persistence and escalation
Names concrete process, auth, and filesystem artifacts
Prioritizes containment without destroying evidence
Uses service isolation and least privilege in prevention

quiz.prompt: Which statement best distinguishes DAC from MAC?
options: DAC is encrypted; MAC is hashed
DAC lets object owners influence permissions; MAC enforces centrally defined labels or policy
quiz.explanation: DAC commonly permits owner-controlled access, while MAC applies centrally enforced policy.

quiz.prompt: Why collect process and network state early in a host investigation?
options: They are usually more volatile than disk artifacts
Disk images include no process information
quiz.explanation: Live process, connection, and memory state can disappear during shutdown or containment.

modules.title: Web Application Security
modules.why: Browser trust boundaries, untrusted input, session authority, and backend interpreters create recurring high-impact failures.
objectives: Trace browser, API, and server trust boundaries
Compare injection, XSS, CSRF, SSRF, and access-control failures
Design layered fixes instead of single-filter patches

concepts.title: Authority and interpretation
concepts.body: Ask who supplies data, which component interprets it, and whose authority the resulting action uses. This model unifies injection, SSRF, CSRF, and confused-deputy failures.

concepts.title: Authorization belongs server-side
concepts.body: Authentication establishes identity; every object and action still requires authorization independent of client controls.

concepts.title: Fix the class
concepts.body: Prefer parameterization, contextual output encoding, safe APIs, allowlisted destinations, and centralized authorization over payload blacklists.

modules.example: A URL-fetch feature can become SSRF when the server follows user-controlled destinations with internal network authority. Validate schemes and destinations, block private ranges, constrain redirects, and isolate egress.
pitfalls: Describing CORS as an authorization control
Fixing injection with a blacklist instead of removing unsafe interpretation

lab.title: Threat-model a file upload
lab.scenario: Users upload images that are transformed, scanned, stored, and shared by URL.
deliverables: Draw the trust boundaries and data flow
Find at least six threats across upload, parsing, storage, and retrieval
Choose controls and logs for the three highest-risk threats

interview.prompt: Explain how you would secure a URL-preview service against SSRF.
rubric: Identifies server authority and internal destinations
Covers parsing, redirects, DNS changes, and alternate address forms
Uses egress isolation and least privilege
Adds useful logging and abuse controls

quiz.prompt: What is the core security failure in IDOR/BOLA?
options: Server-side object authorization is absent or incorrect
quiz.explanation: The server permits access to an object without validating the caller's authorization for it.

quiz.prompt: Which control most directly prevents SQL injection?
quiz.explanation: Parameterized queries separate data from SQL syntax.

modules.title: API Security and Abuse Resistance
modules.why: Modern systems expose business authority and sensitive data through APIs, where authorization and workflow abuse dominate.
objectives: Apply object, property, and function authorization
Control resource consumption and sensitive business flows
Secure outbound API consumption and maintain inventory

concepts.title: Authorize every dimension
concepts.body: Check the caller, action, object, property, tenant, and workflow state. Endpoint-level authentication alone misses most API authorization failures.

concepts.title: Abuse can be valid syntax
concepts.body: Scalping, account creation, scraping, and enumeration may use legitimate requests at harmful scale. Model business limits, not only malformed input.

concepts.title: Treat integrations as untrusted
concepts.body: Third-party responses need timeouts, size limits, schema validation, redirect policy, and safe downstream handling.

modules.example: A user can change another customer's shipping address by swapping an object ID. The correct fix is centralized object authorization and tenant scoping, supported by negative tests and audit logs.
pitfalls: Testing only unauthenticated requests
Trusting third-party API data because the provider is reputable

lab.title: Review an order API
lab.scenario: An API supports order lookup, cancellation, address updates, exports, and webhook integrations.
deliverables: Create an authorization matrix for roles, objects, properties, and actions
Identify abuse cases and resource limits
Write five negative security tests including cross-tenant access

interview.prompt: Design authorization for a multi-tenant document API.
rubric: Separates authentication from authorization
Models tenant, object, property, and action checks
Uses deny-by-default centralized policy
Covers enumeration resistance, auditability, and tests

quiz.prompt: Which flaw allows a user to read another tenant's record by changing an ID?
quiz.explanation: The server failed to verify authorization for the referenced object.

quiz.prompt: What best mitigates unsafe consumption of a third-party API?
options: Trust responses over TLS without validation
Validate schemas, constrain redirects, limit resources, and set timeouts
Expose the integration directly to browsers
quiz.explanation: Transport security does not make remote data trustworthy or bounded.

modules.title: Applied Cryptography
modules.why: Security engineers must select primitives by security goal and recognize implementation failures without inventing cryptography.
objectives: Map primitives to confidentiality, integrity, and authenticity
Explain key agreement, authenticated encryption, hashing, and signatures
Recognize nonce, randomness, key, and downgrade failures

concepts.title: Start with the property
concepts.body: Encoding changes representation. Hashing creates a one-way digest. Encryption protects confidentiality. MACs and signatures provide different forms of authenticity.

concepts.title: Protocols compose primitives
concepts.body: TLS combines authentication, key agreement, symmetric authenticated encryption, transcript integrity, and downgrade protection.

concepts.title: Key lifecycle is the system
concepts.body: Generation, storage, access, rotation, revocation, backup, and destruction often matter more than algorithm trivia.

modules.example: For stored API credentials, use a managed key service, envelope encryption, scoped decrypt permission, rotation, audit logs, and a revocation plan rather than embedding a key in configuration.
pitfalls: Calling base64 encryption
Choosing an algorithm without designing key and nonce lifecycle

lab.title: Design secret storage
lab.scenario: A service must store customer integration tokens and decrypt them during jobs.
deliverables: Draw key hierarchy and trust boundaries
Define access, rotation, revocation, and audit behavior
Explain blast radius for three compromise scenarios

interview.prompt: Why does TLS use both asymmetric and symmetric cryptography?
rubric: Explains authentication and key agreement
Explains symmetric performance
Mentions transcript integrity and authenticated encryption
Avoids claiming RSA simply encrypts all traffic

quiz.prompt: Which primitive is appropriate for storing user passwords?
options: Reversible encryption with a global key
quiz.explanation: Passwords should use a salted, deliberately expensive password-hashing function.

quiz.prompt: What can happen if a nonce is reused with certain AEAD modes?
options: Confidentiality and integrity can fail catastrophically
quiz.explanation: Nonce uniqueness is a core requirement for common AEAD constructions.

modules.title: Authentication and Session Security
modules.why: Account takeover and token theft turn authentication design mistakes into durable attacker access.
objectives: Compare sessions, bearer tokens, federation, and phishing-resistant MFA
Model token issuance, storage, rotation, revocation, and recovery
Distinguish authentication, authorization, and session management

concepts.title: Credentials are capabilities
concepts.body: Cookies, bearer tokens, refresh tokens, API keys, and certificates grant authority when presented. Protect them according to lifetime and scope.

concepts.title: Authentication is a lifecycle
concepts.body: Enrollment, login, step-up, recovery, device changes, revocation, and deprovisioning each create attack paths.

concepts.title: Phishing resistance is protocol-level
concepts.body: WebAuthn/passkeys bind authentication to the legitimate origin and resist replay better than codes or approval prompts.

modules.example: After refresh-token theft, changing a password may be insufficient. Revoke token families, inspect issuance and use, terminate sessions, and fix the storage path that exposed it.
pitfalls: Treating JWT validation as authorization
Building strong login while leaving account recovery weak

lab.title: Review an auth system
lab.scenario: A SaaS app supports passwords, passkeys, SSO, recovery email, and long-lived mobile sessions.
deliverables: Draw credential and token lifecycles
Threat-model enrollment, recovery, and revocation
Define logs and response steps for token theft

interview.prompt: Design authentication for an administrative console.
rubric: Clarifies threat model and user population
Uses phishing-resistant MFA and secure recovery
Scopes sessions and high-risk step-up
Covers revocation, audit logs, and break-glass access

quiz.prompt: What property makes WebAuthn more phishing-resistant than one-time codes?
options: Credentials are bound to the relying-party origin
It stores the private key on the server
quiz.explanation: Origin binding prevents a credential assertion from being replayed to a lookalike origin.

quiz.prompt: What must happen when validating a JWT?
options: Verify algorithm, signature, issuer, audience, and relevant time claims
Trust any token with three segments
Use its subject as authorization without policy
quiz.explanation: A decoded token is not trusted until its cryptographic and semantic claims are validated.

modules.title: Identity, IAM, and Privilege Graphs
modules.why: Enterprise and cloud compromise often follows indirect identity edges rather than a software exploit.
objectives: Model users, workloads, roles, resources, and delegation as a graph
Find privilege-escalation and impersonation paths
Design least privilege, review, and break-glass controls

concepts.title: Permissions compose
concepts.body: A harmless-looking permission can become powerful when chained with token creation, role modification, secret access, or workload deployment.

concepts.title: Human and workload identity differ
concepts.body: Automation needs short-lived, attributable workload identity rather than exported user-like credentials.

concepts.title: Lifecycle prevents drift
concepts.body: Provisioning, access review, separation of duties, just-in-time elevation, and deprovisioning limit accumulated privilege.

modules.example: A CI principal cannot read production directly but can modify a deployment that runs as a production service account. That deployment edge is an effective privilege-escalation path.
pitfalls: Reviewing roles individually instead of traversing privilege paths
Using long-lived service-account keys where workload federation is available

lab.title: Find the privilege path
lab.scenario: A developer can edit a pipeline, the pipeline deploys a workload, and the workload can read production secrets.
deliverables: Draw the identity and permission graph
Identify the shortest escalation path and required conditions
Propose preventive, detective, and recovery controls

interview.prompt: How would you secure service accounts in a cloud environment?
rubric: Prefers short-lived workload identity
Scopes permissions and trust relationships
Covers key inventory and rotation where keys remain
Includes monitoring, review, and incident revocation

quiz.prompt: Why can permission to deploy a workload be equivalent to service-account impersonation?
options: The workload may execute using that service account's authority
Deployment permissions automatically grant billing access
quiz.explanation: Controlling code that runs under a privileged identity can provide its effective authority.

quiz.prompt: What most reduces risk from exported service-account keys?
options: Replacing them with short-lived workload identity where possible
quiz.explanation: Short-lived federated identity removes durable exported credentials and improves attribution.

modules.title: Cloud, Containers, and Kubernetes
modules.why: Cloud control planes, workload identity, orchestration, and shared kernels create distinct attack paths and containment boundaries.
objectives: Compare VM, container, cluster, and cloud-account boundaries
Secure Kubernetes identity, workloads, network, and admission
Trace metadata, control-plane, image, and cross-account attack paths

concepts.title: Control plane is production authority
concepts.body: Cloud and Kubernetes APIs can create workloads, attach identities, read secrets, and change policy. Protect their credentials as privileged code execution.

concepts.title: Containers share a kernel
concepts.body: Namespaces, cgroups, capabilities, seccomp, MAC, and runtime configuration reduce risk but do not create a VM boundary.

concepts.title: Default paths matter
concepts.body: Metadata endpoints, mounted tokens, permissive RBAC, public control planes, mutable images, and open egress turn one workload compromise into a platform incident.

modules.example: An SSRF bug reaches a metadata service, steals workload credentials, lists secrets, and creates a privileged pod. Prevent each edge with destination controls, scoped identity, RBAC, admission policy, and network isolation.
pitfalls: Calling a container a security boundary without qualification
Focusing on workload CVEs while ignoring control-plane authorization

lab.title: Harden a Kubernetes workload
lab.scenario: A public API pod runs as root, mounts a broad service-account token, has unrestricted egress, and uses a mutable image tag.
deliverables: Rank the weaknesses by plausible attack path
Write a hardened workload and policy checklist
Define audit and runtime signals for attempted escalation

interview.prompt: A container is compromised. How do you determine whether the cluster is compromised too?
rubric: Scopes identity, mounts, capabilities, host access, and network reach
Inspects API audit and cloud control-plane logs
Checks persistence and other workloads
Contains credentials and workload while preserving evidence

quiz.prompt: Which Kubernetes condition most directly enables host compromise?
options: A privileged pod mounting the host filesystem
quiz.explanation: Privileged host-mounted workloads can cross the container boundary and modify the node.

quiz.prompt: Why should default service-account token mounting be minimized?
options: A compromised workload may use the token against the Kubernetes API
quiz.explanation: Mounted credentials create a control-plane attack path from workload compromise.

modules.title: Secure SDLC and Software Supply Chain
modules.why: Product security reduces vulnerability classes and protects the systems that build, sign, and distribute software.
objectives: Place security requirements and verification throughout delivery
Threat-model source, CI/CD, dependencies, artifacts, and deployment
Use provenance, review, isolation, and secure defaults to reduce systemic risk

concepts.title: Secure the factory
concepts.body: Developer endpoints, source control, CI runners, package sources, artifact stores, signing keys, and deployment identities form a high-value production system.

concepts.title: Prevent classes
concepts.body: Safe frameworks, memory-safe languages, centralized authorization, secure defaults, and paved roads remove recurring defects more effectively than repeated findings.

concepts.title: Evidence travels with artifacts
concepts.body: Pinned dependencies, SBOMs, provenance, signatures, isolated builds, and policy verification help establish what was built and from which inputs.

modules.example: A stolen CI token modifies a release and signs it with a broadly accessible key. Separate build and signing authority, isolate runners, require reviewed changes, use short-lived identity, and verify provenance at deployment.
pitfalls: Treating SAST as the secure development program
Collecting an SBOM without ownership or vulnerability response

lab.title: Threat-model a release pipeline
lab.scenario: Pull requests trigger third-party actions, build containers, sign images, and deploy automatically.
deliverables: Draw identities, trust boundaries, inputs, and artifacts
Find at least eight tampering or credential paths
Design controls for review, runner isolation, provenance, signing, and deployment policy

interview.prompt: How would you improve security across a large engineering organization?
rubric: Starts with risk and developer workflow
Prioritizes reusable paved roads and secure defaults
Combines prevention, verification, and response
Defines adoption and outcome metrics

quiz.prompt: What does artifact provenance primarily help establish?
options: Where and how an artifact was built from declared inputs
That production needs no runtime controls
quiz.explanation: Provenance provides verifiable information about build process and inputs, not a guarantee of bug-free software.

quiz.prompt: Which approach best prevents an entire vulnerability class?
options: Telling every developer to remember a checklist
A secure framework API that makes the unsafe operation unavailable
quiz.explanation: Safe abstractions and defaults remove repeated opportunities to make the same mistake.

modules.title: Vulnerability Management and Remediation
modules.why: Security engineers must turn noisy findings into risk-informed remediation and verify that fixes remove root causes.
objectives: Prioritize using exploitability, exposure, asset value, and compensating controls
Distinguish finding, vulnerability, exploit path, and systemic cause
Drive remediation, exception, verification, and disclosure workflows

concepts.title: Severity is not priority
concepts.body: CVSS describes characteristics; operational priority also depends on reachability, active exploitation, privileges, business impact, and available mitigations.

concepts.title: Ownership closes findings
concepts.body: Every finding needs an owner, deadline, evidence, exception path, and verification method.

concepts.title: Fix roots, not instances
concepts.body: After patching one endpoint, find sibling paths, add regression tests, improve frameworks, and measure recurrence.

modules.example: A critical library CVE in an unreachable test image may rank below a high-severity internet-facing flaw with known exploitation. Document the decision and continuously re-evaluate exposure.
pitfalls: Sorting the backlog only by CVSS
Closing tickets when code merges rather than when remediation is deployed and verified

lab.title: Prioritize a vulnerability queue
lab.scenario: You have ten findings across an exposed gateway, internal admin tool, dormant image, and developer laptop fleet.
deliverables: Define a transparent prioritization model
Rank the findings and defend the top three
Specify remediation evidence, deadlines, and exception criteria

interview.prompt: Engineering cannot fix every vulnerability this quarter. What do you do?
rubric: Builds a risk model beyond scanner severity
Identifies active exploitation and critical paths
Uses compensating controls and explicit exceptions
Measures time-to-remediate and recurrence

quiz.prompt: Which factor most directly changes practical urgency beyond base severity?
options: Known exploitation on an internet-reachable asset
The number of comments on the ticket
quiz.explanation: Active exploitation and exposure materially increase practical risk.

quiz.prompt: When is a vulnerability remediation complete?
options: When the fix is deployed and independently verified
quiz.explanation: Completion requires deployed remediation and verification, not administrative closure.

modules.title: Threat Modeling and Security Design
modules.why: Threat modeling converts architecture and attacker goals into prioritized design decisions before incidents occur.
objectives: Create data-flow diagrams with identities, assets, and trust boundaries
Generate threats systematically and abuse cases creatively
Prioritize mitigations with explicit assumptions and residual risk

concepts.title: Model the real system
concepts.body: Include control planes, administrators, CI/CD, third parties, support workflows, logs, and recovery paths, not only the request path.

concepts.title: Frameworks prompt thinking
concepts.body: STRIDE and ATT&CK are lenses, not answers. Tie every threat to a concrete component, precondition, impact, and control.

concepts.title: Prioritize decisions
concepts.body: A useful model produces owners and design changes. Record assumptions, residual risk, and triggers for review.

modules.example: For file upload, model parser execution, metadata, decompression, object storage, sharing links, malware scanning, lifecycle deletion, and the identities crossing each boundary.
pitfalls: Producing a generic threat checklist detached from architecture
Assigning subjective scores without explaining likelihood and impact

lab.title: Threat-model a webhook platform
lab.scenario: Customers register URLs; the service sends signed events and retries failures.
deliverables: Draw the complete data flow and trust boundaries
Generate threats for registration, dispatch, signing, retries, and operations
Rank the top five and propose preventive and detective controls

interview.prompt: Threat-model a service that generates image previews from user-supplied URLs.
rubric: Clarifies assets, users, scale, and assumptions
Draws data flow and trust boundaries
Finds SSRF, parser, resource, storage, and abuse risks
Prioritizes layered controls and observability

quiz.prompt: What makes a threat-model finding actionable?
options: It names a STRIDE category only
It connects a concrete path and impact to an owner and mitigation
quiz.explanation: Actionable findings connect system-specific risk to a decision and accountable owner.

quiz.prompt: Which STRIDE category maps most directly to unauthorized privilege gain?
quiz.explanation: Elevation of privilege covers violations of authorization boundaries.

modules.title: Exploitation and Attack Surface
modules.why: Understanding exploit preconditions and primitives helps you reason about impact without memorizing individual CVEs.
objectives: Separate vulnerability, exploit primitive, precondition, and impact
Compare local, remote, client-side, social, and physical paths
Explain shells, persistence, privilege escalation, and egress constraints

concepts.title: Build the chain
concepts.body: An exploit path combines reachability, vulnerable behavior, attacker-controlled input, a useful primitive, privileges, and post-exploitation opportunity.

concepts.title: Primitive is not outcome
concepts.body: Read, write, crash, redirect, disclose, and execute primitives have different value depending on process authority and environment.

concepts.title: Environment shapes reliability
concepts.body: Memory protections, versions, architecture, sandboxing, egress, and monitoring determine whether a proof becomes a practical attack.

modules.example: A memory disclosure may expose a session secret, which enables account takeover without code execution. Trace impact from primitive rather than ranking only by exploit label.
pitfalls: Equating every crash with remote code execution
Explaining an exploit without stating reachability and privileges

lab.title: Analyze an exploit chain
lab.scenario: A public parser crashes on crafted input and runs in a sandboxed low-privilege service.
deliverables: List known facts, unknowns, and required preconditions
Describe possible primitives and how you would validate them safely
Map containment layers and telemetry to each step

interview.prompt: What is the difference between a reverse shell and a bind shell, and why does it matter?
rubric: Explains connection direction and listener
Connects choice to ingress and egress policy
Discusses process identity and network telemetry
Avoids presenting shell access as automatic root compromise

quiz.prompt: A file-read primitive in a web process is most valuable to an attacker when it can access what?
options: Secrets or credentials enabling another trust boundary
quiz.explanation: Exploit value depends on reachable sensitive data and the next edge in the attack chain.

quiz.prompt: Why might an attacker choose a reverse shell?
options: Outbound connectivity may be allowed when inbound connectivity is blocked
quiz.explanation: Reverse shells initiate outbound connections, which may traverse restrictive ingress environments.

modules.title: Malware Behavior and Analysis
modules.why: Security engineers should extract behavior, capabilities, and durable detection opportunities from suspicious software.
objectives: Choose safe static, dynamic, and memory-analysis approaches
Recognize persistence, injection, evasion, C2, and collection behavior
Turn observations into scoped indicators and behavioral detections

concepts.title: Start safely and cheaply
concepts.body: Hash, identify format, inspect strings and imports, and gather context before executing a sample in an isolated instrumented environment.

concepts.title: Behavior outlives indicators
concepts.body: Domains and hashes rotate quickly; process relationships, credential access, persistence, and protocol patterns can produce more durable detections.

concepts.title: Analysis is hypothesis-driven
concepts.body: Ask what capability you need to confirm, choose instrumentation, and record confidence and alternative explanations.

modules.example: A sample creates a scheduled task, injects into a signed process, and beacons with domain generation. Preserve the sample, observe safely, extract configuration, and detect the behaviors as well as the hash.
pitfalls: Executing an unknown sample on a normal workstation
Publishing broad signatures without checking false positives or provenance

lab.title: Plan safe malware triage
lab.scenario: An attachment was quarantined after execution on one laptop.
deliverables: Write the collection and isolation plan
Choose static and dynamic questions in order
Produce a hypothetical IOC package plus two behavior-based detections

interview.prompt: How would you analyze a suspicious binary you received from incident response?
rubric: Starts with authorization, preservation, and isolation
Uses staged static and dynamic analysis
Connects observations to capabilities and scope
Produces detections with confidence and limitations

quiz.prompt: Why are behavior-based detections often more durable than file hashes?
options: Attackers can change a file hash while retaining core behavior
quiz.explanation: Minor binary changes rotate hashes, while operational behavior may remain stable.

quiz.prompt: What should happen before dynamically executing an unknown sample?
options: Establish an isolated, instrumented environment and preservation plan
quiz.explanation: Dynamic analysis requires controlled isolation, instrumentation, and evidence handling.

modules.title: Attack Lifecycle and ATT&CK Reasoning
modules.why: Lifecycle reasoning keeps broad incident and design answers coherent from initial access through impact.
objectives: Narrate realistic multi-stage attack paths
Map actions to ATT&CK techniques without treating the framework as a checklist
Identify evidence and intervention opportunities at each stage

concepts.title: Attackers pursue objectives
concepts.body: Techniques are means to gain access, authority, knowledge, persistence, collection, or impact. Start from objectives and constraints.

concepts.title: Edges matter
concepts.body: The transition between stages often depends on credentials, trust relationships, network paths, or operational mistakes.

concepts.title: Coverage is layered
concepts.body: Prevent, detect, contain, and recover across the chain. Avoid assuming one blocked technique ends the campaign.

modules.example: A phish steals a session, mailbox rules establish persistence, cloud discovery reveals an app registration, delegated authority enables collection, and a storage service enables exfiltration.
pitfalls: Forcing every incident into a perfectly linear kill chain
Listing ATT&CK IDs without explaining evidence or system context

lab.title: Build an attack narrative
lab.scenario: A contractor account logs in from new infrastructure, creates an OAuth grant, accesses shared drives, and uploads archives.
deliverables: Create a timestamped attack hypothesis
Map each action to objective, technique, evidence, and confidence
Choose containment steps and explain where they could alert the actor

interview.prompt: Walk through a realistic cloud account compromise from initial access to exfiltration.
rubric: States assumptions and target assets
Uses plausible identity and control-plane edges
Names logs and evidence throughout
Includes containment, evasion, and recovery tradeoffs

quiz.prompt: What is the most useful way to apply ATT&CK during investigation?
options: Treat missing technique IDs as proof nothing happened
Use techniques to structure hypotheses, evidence, and coverage
Replace the incident timeline with the matrix
Assume all actors use every tactic
quiz.explanation: ATT&CK organizes behavior and hypotheses; it does not replace evidence or context.

quiz.prompt: Which artifact best supports persistence through OAuth consent?
quiz.explanation: A malicious or unexpected application grant can preserve API access beyond an interactive session.

modules.title: Mitigations and Security Architecture
modules.why: Strong candidates choose controls that break concrete attack paths while preserving system usability and reliability.
objectives: Map controls to specific threats and failure points
Use prevention, detection, response, and recovery together
Explain residual risk, cost, usability, and operational tradeoffs

concepts.title: Controls need mechanisms
concepts.body: Do not say 'validate input' or 'use least privilege' alone. State where, against what policy, with which failure behavior, and how it is tested.

concepts.title: Layer independent failures
concepts.body: Defense in depth is useful when layers fail differently, not when five products depend on the same identity or configuration.

concepts.title: Prefer secure defaults
concepts.body: Move responsibility toward platform design so normal use is safe and exceptions are explicit, narrow, temporary, and visible.

modules.example: For credential theft, combine phishing-resistant authentication, scoped sessions, device and risk signals, secure recovery, rapid revocation, and investigation telemetry.
pitfalls: Naming a control without the attack step it breaks
Adding user friction when a system-level safe default is available

lab.title: Design layered mitigations
lab.scenario: A legacy parser handles untrusted documents with a history of memory-corruption bugs.
deliverables: Map the attack path and highest-impact assets
Design independent preventive and containment layers
Define residual risk, monitoring, rollback, and long-term replacement

interview.prompt: What mitigations would you use for memory-corruption risk in a network service?
rubric: Covers elimination or memory-safe replacement
Uses patching, compiler, OS, sandbox, and privilege controls
Addresses exposure and egress
Includes detection and operational tradeoffs

quiz.prompt: Which is the best example of independent defense in depth?
options: Two alerts fed by the same missing log
Input-safe parsing plus sandboxing plus low-privilege execution
quiz.explanation: These controls act at different failure points and reduce impact if one fails.

quiz.prompt: What should accompany a temporary security exception?
options: Owner, scope, compensating controls, expiry, and review
quiz.explanation: Explicit ownership, bounds, controls, and expiration prevent exceptions from silently becoming defaults.

modules.title: Detection Engineering and Threat Hunting
modules.why: Detection engineering turns attacker behavior and available telemetry into testable, maintainable signals.
objectives: Write detections with hypothesis, data requirements, logic, and tests
Measure false positives, coverage, latency, and operational value
Use threat hunting to validate visibility and discover behavior

concepts.title: Detection is software
concepts.body: Version logic, test it against positive and negative fixtures, review changes, monitor data quality, and assign ownership.

concepts.title: Context creates fidelity
concepts.body: Identity, asset criticality, process ancestry, destination rarity, and sequence often outperform a single IOC.

concepts.title: Design for evasion
concepts.body: Ask how an attacker slows down, distributes activity, uses valid accounts, tampers with telemetry, or blends into administration.

modules.example: A password-spray detector counts failures across many users per source, suppresses known identity tests, raises severity after a success, and joins device and geolocation context.
pitfalls: Alerting on every suspicious event without a response decision
Ignoring data loss, schema changes, and delayed ingestion

lab.title: Write and test a detection
lab.scenario: Authentication logs include timestamp, source, user, result, device, and application.
deliverables: Define a password-spray hypothesis and required fields
Write pseudocode or a query with positive and negative fixtures
Document triage steps, false positives, evasion, and health metrics

interview.prompt: Design a detector for DNS exfiltration.
rubric: Clarifies resolver and endpoint telemetry
Combines length, entropy, uniqueness, volume, and rarity
Builds baselines and benign exclusions
Covers process attribution, evasion, testing, and triage

quiz.prompt: What makes a detection production-ready?
options: It fires on one attack sample
It has data contracts, tests, triage guidance, ownership, and health monitoring
quiz.explanation: Operational quality requires reliable data, validated logic, response context, and lifecycle ownership.

quiz.prompt: Why join a successful login after repeated failures?
options: Success proves the user is malicious
It can raise confidence that guessing or spraying achieved access
quiz.explanation: A success after suspicious failures can indicate progression from attempt to account compromise.

modules.title: Incident Response and Command
modules.why: Incident interviews test technical judgment, prioritization, communication, evidence handling, and recovery under uncertainty.
objectives: Run preparation, identification, containment, eradication, recovery, and learning
Assign command, operations, communications, and documentation roles
Balance rapid containment with evidence, business, legal, and attacker-awareness concerns

concepts.title: Stabilize the system
concepts.body: Define incident scope, severity, objectives, roles, cadence, source of truth, and decision authority early.

concepts.title: Contain with hypotheses
concepts.body: Actions should break likely attacker access while preserving evidence and anticipating alternate persistence.

concepts.title: Recovery proves trust
concepts.body: Rotate affected authority, rebuild where needed, validate controls, monitor recurrence, and communicate residual uncertainty.

modules.example: For stolen cloud credentials, revoke active tokens and persistence grants, preserve control-plane logs, scope resource access, rotate downstream secrets, validate recovery, and investigate the original theft path.
pitfalls: Immediately reimaging the only affected host before volatile collection
Confusing the first alert with root cause or full scope

lab.title: Command a cloud incident
lab.scenario: A privileged account created new credentials and accessed sensitive storage before the alert fired.
deliverables: Write the first 30-minute objectives, roles, and decisions
Build a containment and evidence plan with sequencing
Draft executive update, recovery criteria, and lessons-learned questions

interview.prompt: You suspect a senior engineer's account is compromised. Walk through the incident.
rubric: Establishes severity, roles, and communication
Preserves evidence and scopes identity activity
Contains sessions, credentials, and persistence
Handles privacy, business impact, recovery, and root cause

quiz.prompt: Why can immediate containment be risky?
options: It may destroy evidence or alert the actor before scope is understood
quiz.explanation: Containment timing should weigh ongoing harm, evidence, scope, and attacker reaction.

quiz.prompt: What distinguishes root cause from symptom?
options: Root cause is the first alert
Root cause explains the underlying failure that enabled the incident
Root cause is always one person
quiz.explanation: Root cause addresses the enabling systemic or technical failure, not only its visible effect.

modules.title: Digital Forensics and Timelines
modules.why: Forensics provides disciplined methods to preserve, recover, correlate, and explain evidence during an investigation.
objectives: Prioritize collection by volatility and investigative value
Build timelines across host, network, identity, and cloud artifacts
Preserve provenance, integrity, and chain of custody

concepts.title: Question drives collection
concepts.body: Collect evidence that can confirm or refute hypotheses while accounting for volatility, cost, authority, and system impact.

concepts.title: Time needs normalization
concepts.body: Record source clock, timezone, skew, ingestion delay, and timestamp semantics before correlating events.

concepts.title: Preservation supports trust
concepts.body: Document who collected what, when, how, with which tools and hashes, and every transfer or transformation.

modules.example: A timeline joins identity-provider login, cloud audit, endpoint process, browser history, and object-storage access while recording clock skew and confidence for each event.
pitfalls: Assuming log ingestion time equals event time
Collecting everything without an investigative question or legal authority

lab.title: Construct an incident timeline
lab.scenario: You receive endpoint, authentication, DNS, and cloud events with different timestamp formats and delays.
deliverables: Normalize timestamps and document assumptions
Create a timeline separating facts from hypotheses
Identify missing evidence and the next three investigative pivots

interview.prompt: How would you investigate suspected data theft from an employee laptop?
rubric: Clarifies authority, scope, and data sensitivity
Prioritizes volatile and remote evidence
Builds a multi-source timeline
Preserves chain of custody and avoids overclaiming attribution

quiz.prompt: Why document clock skew in a forensic timeline?
options: To avoid incorrect ordering and causal conclusions
quiz.explanation: Different clocks and ingestion delays can create misleading event order.

quiz.prompt: What does chain of custody establish?
options: Documented control and handling of evidence over time
That no forensic tool has bugs
quiz.explanation: Chain of custody records possession, transfer, and handling so evidence integrity can be evaluated.

modules.title: Security Coding and Algorithms
modules.why: Coding is a continuous interview bar: candidates must turn vague security data problems into correct, tested, explainable programs.
objectives: Clarify inputs, outputs, constraints, and abuse cases
Use appropriate collections, parsing, sorting, search, and graph traversal
Test edge cases and explain time and space complexity

concepts.title: Model before typing
concepts.body: Restate the problem, define examples, choose data structures, write pseudocode, and identify complexity before implementation.

concepts.title: Security data is messy
concepts.body: Expect malformed rows, mixed timezones, missing fields, duplicates, untrusted sizes, and adversarial input.

concepts.title: Communicate the tradeoff
concepts.body: A correct simple solution with tests and clear complexity is stronger than an unfinished clever solution.

modules.example: For failed-login bursts, parse records defensively, group by source and sliding window, retain only necessary timestamps, test boundaries, then discuss distributed evasion.
pitfalls: Coding silently before agreeing on requirements
Ignoring malformed input, memory bounds, and test cases

lab.title: Implement an auth-log detector
lab.scenario: Read newline-delimited auth events and flag sources exceeding a failure threshold within a rolling window.
deliverables: Write examples, assumptions, and pseudocode
Implement the detector with malformed-input handling
Add tests and explain complexity plus distributed-attack limitations

interview.prompt: Given logs, return users who authenticated from more than three countries in one hour.
rubric: Clarifies ordering, timestamp, and geolocation assumptions
Chooses suitable maps and window state
Tests duplicates, boundaries, and malformed records
Explains complexity and data-quality limitations

quiz.prompt: What is the average lookup complexity of a well-behaved hash map?
quiz.explanation: Hash maps provide average constant-time lookup, with caveats for collisions and implementation.

quiz.prompt: What should happen before coding an ambiguous interview problem?
options: Clarify requirements and work through examples
quiz.explanation: Clarification and examples expose requirements and guide a correct data model.

modules.title: Security Engineering Projects
modules.why: Small, finished defensive tools provide evidence that you can frame, build, test, and explain security work.
objectives: Define bounded defensive tools with clear inputs and outputs
Use fixtures, tests, metrics, and safe execution boundaries
Explain limitations, false positives, and operational next steps

concepts.title: Choose an observable problem
concepts.body: A useful project answers a concrete question: which hosts scanned, which records violate policy, or which artifacts match a rule.

concepts.title: Fixtures make work credible
concepts.body: Include representative positive, negative, malformed, and boundary cases with deterministic expected results.

concepts.title: Safety is part of design
concepts.body: Keep scanners and credential simulations on authorized localhost fixtures. Prefer detection and analysis objectives over uncontrolled offensive automation.

modules.example: Build a web-log analyzer that flags path enumeration, includes sample logs and unit tests, reports why each alert fired, and documents evasion and false positives.
pitfalls: Building offensive automation without authorization boundaries
Publishing unfinished code without fixtures, tests, or explanation

lab.title: Ship one portfolio artifact
lab.scenario: Choose a log parser, scan detector, metadata extractor, policy checker, or signature matcher.
deliverables: Define threat model, input contract, output, and safety boundary
Implement a tested minimum version with fixtures
Write a concise README covering design, complexity, limitations, and next iteration

interview.prompt: Walk me through a security tool you built and one decision you would change.
rubric: Frames the user and security problem
Explains architecture and key tradeoffs
Shows tests, evidence, and limitations
Reflects honestly on improvement and operations

quiz.prompt: What most improves the interview value of a small security project?
options: Clear scope, fixtures, tests, tradeoffs, and limitations
quiz.explanation: Evidence of disciplined engineering matters more than project size or novelty.

quiz.prompt: Where should a port-scanning exercise run?
options: Only against explicitly authorized local or lab targets
Anywhere if the rate is low
quiz.explanation: Active testing requires explicit authorization and controlled scope.

modules.title: Interview Execution
modules.why: Interviewers can evaluate only the reasoning you expose: assumptions, models, tradeoffs, corrections, and collaboration.
objectives: Structure ambiguous technical and scenario answers
Think aloud without narrating noise
Recover productively when knowledge is incomplete

concepts.title: Clarify the decision
concepts.body: Repeat the problem, identify the goal, ask for constraints and available telemetry, then state remaining assumptions.

concepts.title: Build from first principles
concepts.body: Start with assets, identities, trust boundaries, data flow, attacker goals, and observable behavior before naming products.

concepts.title: Close the loop
concepts.body: Summarize the design, test it against abuse and failure, discuss tradeoffs, and state what you would verify next.

modules.example: For a vague detection prompt: clarify logs and environment, define the behavior, propose simple logic, test benign cases, consider evasion, and describe triage and measurement.
pitfalls: Jumping directly to a favorite tool
Going silent when unsure instead of exposing useful adjacent reasoning

lab.title: Record a mock answer
lab.scenario: Choose one design, detection, incident, and coding prompt from completed modules.
deliverables: Record four timed answers using clarify-model-solve-test-tradeoff
Score each against its module rubric
Rewrite the weakest answer and list three recurring communication habits

interview.prompt: You are asked about a protocol you do not know. What do you do?
rubric: States the knowledge boundary honestly
Connects known adjacent principles
Asks clarifying questions
Builds a tentative model and explains how to verify it

quiz.prompt: What is the best first response to a vague security-design question?
options: Clarify goals, assets, actors, constraints, and assumptions
quiz.explanation: Clarification establishes the system and decision before solutions are evaluated.

quiz.prompt: What should you do when you do not know an exact technical detail?
options: State the gap, reason from known principles, and explain verification
quiz.explanation: Honest boundaries plus first-principles reasoning still provide strong interview signal.

modules.title: Capstone: Compromised SaaS Platform
modules.why: The capstone proves that you can integrate architecture, identity, attack paths, detection, response, coding, and communication.
objectives: Threat-model and harden a realistic SaaS architecture
Investigate a multi-stage identity and API incident
Communicate technical decisions to engineering and leadership

concepts.title: Synthesis over trivia
concepts.body: Strong security engineering connects technical facts to business assets, attacker paths, observable evidence, and practical decisions.

concepts.title: Defend your assumptions
concepts.body: There is no single answer. State constraints, choose priorities, and explain what new evidence would change the plan.

concepts.title: Produce artifacts
concepts.body: A diagram, attack path, detection query, timeline, remediation plan, and executive update make reasoning inspectable.

modules.example: A stolen support session accesses a tenant API, creates an integration token, enumerates storage, and exports data through a trusted workflow. Your job is to design, detect, investigate, contain, and prevent recurrence.
pitfalls: Treating the incident as one isolated bad request
Providing controls without owners, evidence, or validation

lab.title: Complete the interview packet
lab.scenario: A multi-tenant SaaS platform has suspicious support-account activity, a new OAuth integration, cross-tenant API reads, and unusual storage exports.
deliverables: Architecture and trust-boundary diagram plus prioritized threat model
Attack narrative, detection logic, evidence plan, and incident timeline
Containment and root-cause remediation plan with executive update
A tested script that detects one behavior from supplied or self-created fixtures
A 15-minute spoken walkthrough scored against every completed-module rubric

interview.prompt: Present your capstone as a security review followed by an incident response briefing.
rubric: Explains architecture, assets, identities, and assumptions
Connects attack path to evidence and detections
Prioritizes containment and systemic remediation
Communicates uncertainty, tradeoffs, ownership, and metrics

quiz.prompt: Which capstone answer best demonstrates intermediate security engineering?
options: A reasoned attack path tied to evidence, controls, tradeoffs, and owners
A claim that every risk can be eliminated
quiz.explanation: Integrated reasoning and actionable ownership demonstrate engineering judgment.

quiz.prompt: What should happen after the immediate incident is contained?
options: Validate recovery, determine root cause, prevent recurrence, and measure follow-up
quiz.explanation: Recovery and learning complete the response and reduce repeated failure.
